question_text: Match the steps of the Organising Process in Column I with their description in Column II:

column_b.text: Breaking total work into specific jobs
Creating a chain of command (who reports to whom)
Allocating work to individuals based on skills

explanation: (1) Identification and Division of Work = Breaking total work into specific jobs (b). (2) Departmentalisation = Grouping similar activities into departments (a). (3) Assignment of Duties = Allocating work to individuals based on skills (d). (4) Establishing Reporting Relationships = Creating a chain of command (c).
subject: cuet-business-studies

elimination_hints.hint: Division of work means breaking work down (b), not grouping into departments (a). Don't confuse the first two steps.
Departmentalisation is grouping activities (a), not assigning to individuals (d).
Division of work is about breaking work into tasks (b), not allocating to individuals (d) — that comes later.

question_text: Match the features in Column I with the correct type of organisation structure in Column II:

column_a.text: Activities grouped by function (production, marketing, etc.)
Each division operates as an independent profit centre

explanation: (1) Activities grouped by function = Functional Structure (a). (2) Independent profit centres = Divisional Structure (b). (3) Functional specialisation = Functional Structure (a). (4) Duplication of resources = Divisional Structure (b) — because each division has its own functional departments.
subject: cuet-business-studies

elimination_hints.hint: Independent profit centres (2) are a feature of divisional structure, not functional.
Grouping by function (1) is the defining feature of functional structure, not divisional.
This reverses every feature — functional specialisation belongs to functional structure, not divisional.

question_text: Match the features in Column I with the correct type of organisation in Column II:
explanation: (1) Deliberately created by management = Formal (a). (2) Grapevine communication = Informal (b). (3) Social and emotional needs = Informal (b). (4) Clear authority-responsibility = Formal (a).
subject: cuet-business-studies

elimination_hints.hint: Grapevine (2) is informal communication — not formal. Formal uses official channels.
'Deliberately created by management' (1) is the hallmark of formal, not informal organisation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the origin of each type

elimination_hints.hint: Clear authority-responsibility (4) is formal, and deliberately created (1) is also formal — not informal.

question_text: Match the elements of Delegation in Column I with their description in Column II:

column_b.text: Obligation to perform the assigned task
Right to command and make decisions
Answerability for the final outcome (cannot be delegated)

explanation: (1) Authority = Right to command and make decisions (b) — it flows from top to bottom. (2) Responsibility = Obligation to perform the assigned task (a) — it arises when a subordinate accepts a task. (3) Accountability = Answerability for the final outcome (c) — it is absolute and cannot be delegated.
subtopic: Elements of delegation matched with descriptions
subject: cuet-business-studies

elimination_hints.hint: Authority is the 'right to command' (b), not the 'obligation to perform' (a). Obligation is responsibility.
Authority is the right to command (b), not answerability (c). Answerability is accountability.
Authority is the right to command (b), not the obligation to perform (a). Option D swaps authority and responsibility.

question_text: Match the concepts in Column I with the correct description in Column II:

column_b.text: Systematic delegation throughout the organisation as a policy
Granting authority from superior to subordinate for specific tasks
Concentration of decision-making at the top level

explanation: (1) Delegation = Granting authority from superior to subordinate (c). (2) Decentralisation = Systematic delegation throughout the organisation (a). (3) Centralisation = Concentration of decision-making at top (d). (4) Departmentalisation = Grouping similar activities into departments (b).
subject: cuet-business-studies

elimination_hints.hint: Delegation (1) is a one-to-one superior-subordinate act (c), not organisation-wide (a). Organisation-wide is decentralisation.
Decentralisation (2) is systematic delegation (a), not concentration at top (d). Concentration at top is centralisation.
Delegation (1) is granting authority (c), not concentration at top (d). Concentration at top is centralisation.
